title: Listening - Part 4: Teaching Circus Skills
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Ben, a circus-skills teacher. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Ben Walsh, who teaches circus skills to children and adults. Ben, did you grow up wanting to perform in a circus? || Ben: I saw circus shows as a child, but I was quite shy and never imagined being on stage. At university a friend persuaded me to try a juggling club. [24]I liked the fact that a trick which seemed impossible became achievable through small repeated steps[/24]. That feeling was addictive, and eventually I trained in acrobatics and performance as well. || Presenter: Why did you move from performing to teaching? || Ben: I still perform occasionally, but travelling from venue to venue could be lonely. During one tour I ran a workshop at a youth centre. [25]A quiet child who had refused to join in finally balanced a spinning plate and suddenly wanted to show everybody[/25]. I found that moment more rewarding than applause for my own show. || Presenter: What skill do beginners usually want to learn first? || Ben: Juggling, because they have seen it and it looks impressive. But I often begin with balance or throwing one scarf. [26]Using light scarves slows everything down and gives beginners enough time to understand where their hands should move[/26]. Once they experience success, balls no longer seem so impossible. || Presenter: Are parents ever concerned about safety? || Ben: Naturally, particularly when they hear words such as aerial or acrobatics. We use low equipment and mats, and nobody attempts a new movement without preparation. [27]I explain that learning how to fall safely and recognise one's limits comes before attempting any dramatic trick[/27]. Controlled risk is part of learning, but it is carefully managed. || Presenter: You teach adults too. Is that different? || Ben: Adults often apologise before they have even begun. They say they are uncoordinated or too old, while children simply try. [28]The biggest obstacle for adult beginners is usually embarrassment about making mistakes in front of other people[/28]. Once a group laughs together rather than at anyone, their progress is rapid. || Presenter: Does circus training offer benefits beyond physical skill? || Ben: Absolutely. In pair acrobatics, people must communicate clearly and trust each other. Juggling also requires persistence. [29]Many young participants become more willing to try difficult school tasks after discovering that dropping an object is a normal stage of improvement[/29]. Failure becomes information, not a reason to stop. || Presenter: What project are you planning now? || Ben: We are preparing a show involving different age groups from the neighbourhood. It will not be a polished professional production, and that is the point. [30]I want grandparents, teenagers and children to create a performance together rather than taking part in separate classes[/30]. A shared goal can connect people who might not otherwise meet.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What originally attracted Ben to circus skills?

options.A: The chance to appear in public.
options.B: The gradual process of mastering tricks.
options.C: The opportunity to travel with friends.

questions.explanation: He enjoyed achieving an apparently impossible trick through repeated small steps.

questions.question: Why did Ben become more interested in teaching?

options.A: He enjoyed seeing a hesitant child gain confidence.
options.B: He became tired of learning difficult skills.
options.C: He wanted to work in a youth centre permanently.

questions.explanation: A shy child's delight at succeeding was more rewarding than applause.

questions.question: Why does Ben use scarves with beginners?

options.A: They are safer for outdoor practice.
options.B: They make a performance look more colourful.
options.C: Their slow movement makes learning easier.

questions.explanation: Scarves fall slowly, giving beginners time to follow the movement.

questions.question: How does Ben respond to concerns about safety?

options.A: He avoids teaching acrobatics to children.
options.B: He emphasises preparation and safe falling.
options.C: He asks parents to supervise every lesson.

questions.explanation: Recognising limits and learning to fall safely precede dramatic tricks.

questions.question: What difficulty do adult students often have?

options.C: Finding enough time for regular practice.

questions.explanation: Ben says adult beginners are frequently embarrassed to make mistakes in front of others.

questions.question: How can circus training help young people generally?

options.A: It teaches them to compete effectively.
options.C: It can change their attitude to difficult tasks.

questions.explanation: They learn that mistakes are normal during improvement and become more willing to try.

questions.question: What is the aim of Ben's new project?

options.B: To bring different generations together in one show.
options.C: To create a professional travelling production.

questions.explanation: He wants grandparents, teenagers and children to produce a performance together.
